items.text: Chapter One
General Introduction to the Tomato Crop and the Threat of Tuta absoluta
1. General Introduction

Tomatoes are among the most important food and economic vegetable crops worldwide because of their extensive cultivation, their many forms of consumption, and their use in numerous food industries. They are consumed not only fresh, but are also used to produce tomato paste, juices, sauces, ketchup, peeled and canned tomatoes, powders, concentrates, and other products.

Tomatoes occupy a prominent position within agricultural systems because they are a relatively high-value crop with rapid economic turnover and intensive labor and input requirements. They are also linked to a broad chain of activities that includes seed and seedling production, fertilizers and pesticides, crop operations, harvesting, transport, grading, packaging, marketing, and food processing.

However, this major economic importance makes the tomato crop highly sensitive to any decline in yield or quality. Exposure to a serious insect pest does not merely mean losing some leaves or fruits; it can also reduce marketable yield, increase control costs, cause some consignments to be rejected, raise the proportion of losses, and disrupt supply schedules for factories and markets.

Among the pests that have radically transformed tomato production systems worldwide in recent decades, Tuta absoluta—known as the tomato leaf miner or tomato borer—stands out as one of the most dangerous invasive pests threatening tomato production in both open fields and protected cultivation.
2. Global Importance of Tomatoes
2.1 Global Production Volume

Tomatoes are grown in most agricultural regions worldwide, from temperate and Mediterranean areas to tropical and subtropical zones. According to statistics from the Food and Agriculture Organization, global tomato production reached approximately 192 million tonnes in 2023, ranking tomatoes first among vegetable crops in terms of worldwide production volume that year. [1]

This enormous production volume reflects the importance of tomatoes to global food security and shows how sensitive markets are to major disruptions in key production areas, whether caused by pests, diseases, heat waves, water shortages, or supply-chain disturbances.
The importance of tomatoes is not limited to countries that produce them for domestic consumption. Some countries depend on tomatoes as:

items: A crop for the fresh export market.
A source of agricultural and industrial employment.
An important component of food security.
A raw material for tomato paste and sauce factories.
A strategic crop in regional and international trade.

items.text: 2.2 Nutritional Value
Tomato fruits contain a range of important nutritional and health-related compounds, including:

items: A range of phenolic compounds and antioxidants.

items.text: Lycopene is one of the most important compounds associated with the red color of tomato fruits and is also a major quality indicator in tomato cultivars intended for processing. Its value and concentration are affected by the cultivar, ripening stage, temperature, nutrition, and the health status of the plant and fruit.
Tomato quality is not limited to nutritional value. It also includes color, firmness, size, shape, soluble-solids content, acidity, and freedom from insect and disease damage.
2.3 Processing Importance
The production objectives for processing tomatoes differ from those for tomatoes intended for fresh consumption. Processing plants place greater emphasis on characteristics such as:

items: Fruit firmness and ability to withstand transport.
A high proportion of fruit ripening at the same time.
Low losses during grading and processing.

items.text: Insect infestation directly affects these characteristics. An infested fruit may be rejected even when its size and color are suitable because the presence of mines, holes, or larvae inside the fruit makes it commercially and industrially unacceptable.
Insect damage also opens the way for secondary rot pathogens, increasing the proportion of damaged fruit, raising grading costs, and reducing the quantity of raw material suitable for processing.
2.4 Social and Economic Importance
The value of the tomato crop extends beyond the price of the fruit sold, as it supports large numbers of workers in:

Consequently, any major crisis in tomato production transmits its effects from the field to the other links in the value chain. Farmers’ income may decline, some employment may stop, factory operating efficiency may fall, consumer prices may rise, or the need to import processed products may increase.
3. Importance of Tomatoes in Egypt
3.1 The Position of Tomatoes in Egyptian Agriculture

Tomatoes are among Egypt’s most important vegetable crops in terms of area, production, and consumption. Climatic conditions and the diversity of production areas allow them to be grown in multiple seasons and planting windows, keeping the crop available in markets for long periods of the year.
Tomato cultivation is distributed across many areas, including:

An earlier Food and Agriculture Organization assessment of Egypt’s tomato value chain reported national production of about 7.9 million tonnes in 2016, placing Egypt fifth among tomato-producing countries at that time. This figure is cited here as a historical indicator of the scale of the sector, not as a fixed figure for every season. [2]
3.2 Multiple Growing Seasons and Production Areas
Egypt’s multiple tomato-growing seasons help maintain supplies to markets and processing plants, but they also create a major pest-management challenge.

When tomato fields of different ages are located close together, pests can move from older fields to newer plantings. Abandoned fields, crop residues, volunteer plants, or wild solanaceous hosts may become continuous sources of pest reproduction and spread.

This phenomenon is sometimes known as the Green Bridge, in which suitable host plants remain available to the pest for long periods, preventing its life cycle from being completely interrupted between seasons.

The problem becomes more evident when a grower stops controlling a heavily infested field before the plants are removed, shredded, and ploughed under; large numbers of adult moths then migrate into neighboring, younger fields.
3.3 Fresh-Market and Processing Tomatoes
Tomatoes are produced in Egypt for two main purposes:

rows.label: Production for the fresh market
rows.value: This focuses on shape, size, color, firmness, tolerance to handling, and freedom from visible fruit defects.

rows.label: Production for processing
rows.value: This focuses on Brix, color, concentration, firmness, uniform ripening, low defect levels, and efficient conversion into paste or concentrates.

items.text: In both systems, Tuta absoluta infestation poses a direct threat. In the fresh market, holes, mines, and deformities lower the market grade of the fruit. In processing tomatoes, infestation increases grading and rejection rates, raises losses, and may cause loads exceeding acceptable defect and infestation limits to be rejected.
3.4 The Impact of Losses on the Value Chain

Tomatoes are naturally relatively perishable and are strongly affected by poor handling, transport delays, high temperatures, and mechanical and insect damage. Crop loss therefore includes not only fruit that falls in the field, but also:

items: Infested fruit that cannot be sold.
Fruit rejected by the processing plant.
Fruit rotted as a result of insect wounds.
Price reductions caused by poor quality.
Loss of the added value that processing could have generated.

items.text: The Food and Agriculture Organization has conducted specific studies on losses in Egypt’s tomato value chain, reflecting the economic importance of reducing losses from the field through to the consumer. [2]
4. Why Are Pests an Economic Threat?
4.1 The Difference Between Pest Presence and Economic Damage

The presence of one insect or a limited number of insects does not necessarily mean that an economic loss requiring chemical intervention has occurred. An economic decision should be linked to pest density, the life stage present, crop age, the proportion of infested plants or fruit, crop value, intervention cost, and the damage expected if no control action is taken.
Pest management distinguishes among three main concepts:

rows.label: Infestation
rows.value: The presence of the pest or its life stages on the plant or within its tissues.

rows.label: Damage
rows.value: The pest’s actual effect on plant tissues, such as mining, feeding, or fruit injury.

rows.label: Economic Loss
rows.value: The financial reduction caused by crop loss, lower quality, or higher production costs.

items.text: A plant may tolerate a limited infestation, while another small infestation occurring at a sensitive time—such as the beginning of fruit set or during fruit filling—may cause a much greater loss.
4.2 Direct Yield Loss
Pests feed on different parts of the plant and may cause:
With Tuta absoluta, damage is not confined to the leaf. Larvae can attack leaves, stems, growing points, and fruit, extending their impact across vegetative growth, yield, and quality.
4.3 Decline in Market Quality
A field may produce a high total weight, yet a large proportion may be unsaleable because of infestation.
This point is extremely important because the true economic measure is not always total yield, but rather:
Marketable yield or yield accepted for processing.
Fruit with holes, larvae, or rot may be included when estimating total field weight, but it does not represent actual income for the grower.

In fresh tomato markets, a small hole in the fruit can sharply reduce its commercial value. In processing plants, infested fruit may increase defects and grading losses and may also increase the microbial load, affecting the safety and quality of the raw material.
4.4 Increased Production Costs
Pests add multiple items to the cost of producing a feddan, including:

items: Cost of removing heavily infested plants.
Cost of repeat spraying when a treatment fails.

items.text: When spraying is random, a grower may incur high costs without achieving real pest control, especially when the pesticide is applied at the wrong time or against a life stage concealed within plant tissue.
4.5 Pesticide Residues and Pre-Harvest Intervals
Excessive or poorly regulated pesticide use may lead to:

items: Failure to comply with the pre-harvest interval.
Rejection of domestic or export consignments.
Difficulty marketing the crop to processors that follow specifications.
Restrictions on the use of certain active ingredients.
Damage to the reputation of the farm or supplying company.

items.text: This issue is especially sensitive in tomatoes because fresh-market cultivars are harvested repeatedly, while processing tomatoes may be approaching a single, large-scale harvest.
4.6 Development of Insecticide Resistance

Resistance develops when individuals that are more tolerant of an insecticide survive and reproduce, causing resistance traits to become more common in the pest population over successive generations.
The risk of resistance increases when:

items: The same active ingredient is repeatedly used.
Products with different trade names but the same mode of action are used.
Rates below the recommendation are applied.
The pest produces many generations during the season.

items.text: Studies have shown that Tuta absoluta can develop resistance to different insecticide groups through changes in target-site sensitivity or an increased ability to detoxify the insecticide. [3]
4.7 Killing Natural Enemies

Broad-spectrum pesticides may eliminate parasitoids and predators naturally present in the field. When natural-enemy densities decline, the agricultural environment loses part of its natural ability to regulate pest populations.
In some cases, pest numbers decline briefly after spraying and then rise rapidly again because of:

items: Migration of new moths from neighboring fields.
Continued presence of eggs and larvae inside mines.
Failure to remove infestation sources from old fields.

items.text: The success of a spray should therefore be evaluated not only by the number of insects killed immediately, but by a sustained decline in the pest population and the preservation of biological balance within the field.
4.8 Economic-Loss Equation
The economic impact of a pest can be expressed in simplified form by the following relationship:
Total economic impact of the pest =
Loss of marketable yield

items: Price reduction due to lower quality

items.text: This shows that the most dangerous pest is not necessarily the one that consumes the greatest amount of plant tissue, but the one that combines rapid reproduction, difficult detection, high control costs, attack on the marketable part of the plant, and the ability to develop resistance.
5. Introduction to the Threat of Tuta absoluta
5.1 What Is Tuta absoluta?

Tuta absoluta is a small lepidopteran insect native to South America. Within a relatively short period, it became a widespread invasive pest threatening tomato cultivation in Europe, Africa, Asia, and the Middle East.
It is known worldwide by names such as:
The name tomato leaf miner is widely used, but it does not describe every form of damage because the larvae attack not only leaves, but also stems, growing points, flowers, and fruit.

The larval stage is responsible for direct damage. The larva mines into plant tissue and feeds in a location that is relatively protected from external conditions and from many surface spray treatments.
5.2 Why Is It One of the Most Dangerous Tomato Pests?
The threat posed by Tuta absoluta results from the combination of several biological and behavioral characteristics in a single pest.
First: Concealed Feeding

For much of its active period, the larva lives within leaf, stem, or fruit tissue. Spray droplets may therefore reach the plant surface without reaching the concealed larva at an effective concentration.
The presence of larvae inside mines also makes early infestation more difficult to detect unless plants are inspected regularly and carefully.
Second: High Reproductive Capacity

The insect can produce successive generations throughout the year when suitable temperatures and host plants are available. Its relatively short life cycle in warm conditions can cause numbers to rise rapidly if an infestation is not detected early.
Third: Attacking Different Crop Stages
Infestation can begin at the seedling stage and continue through vegetative growth, flowering, fruit set, fruit filling, and up to the period close to harvest.
The economic impact varies according to the timing of infestation:

items: Early infestation may weaken growth and crop establishment.
Damage to growing points may deform growth.
Leaf infestation reduces effective leaf area.
Fruit infestation causes a direct loss in the marketable portion of the crop.

items.text: Fourth: Attacking Multiple Parts of the Plant
Larvae can attack:

items: Fruit that has begun to change color.

items.text: This variety of infestation sites makes control more difficult and means that monitoring based on traps alone is insufficient.
Fifth: High Potential Losses

Monitoring protocols and plant-protection reports indicate that severe, unmanaged infestations can cause losses ranging from 80 to 100% under some conditions, in both protected cultivation and open fields. [4]

This figure must be interpreted carefully. It represents the potential limit during severe outbreaks in the absence of effective control and does not mean that every infested field will automatically lose this proportion of its crop.
Actual loss depends on:

items: The type of control program used.

items.text: Sixth: Rapid Spread

Movement of fruit, seedlings, packaging materials, and agricultural trade, together with the insect’s ability to disperse locally, helped Tuta absoluta move from its native range into extensive new areas.
After it was recorded in Spain in 2006, it spread rapidly across Mediterranean countries, Africa, and Asia and became a major threat to tomato production in most areas where it established. [5]
Seventh: Ability to Develop Resistance

Tuta absoluta has a short life cycle and high reproductive capacity, two factors that increase the selection of resistant individuals when insecticides with the same mode of action are repeatedly applied.

Resistance to multiple insecticide groups has been recorded in different pest populations, making rotation among IRAC groups, reduction of chemical selection pressure, and integration of non-chemical controls essential rather than optional. [3]
Eighth: Movement Between Fields
Adult moths may move from old or neglected fields, or from fields where growers have stopped control measures, into neighboring newer fields.
The risk of this migration increases when:

items: Infested plants are left after harvest ends.
Alternative hosts or volunteer plants are widespread.
Traps are not used in production areas.
Each grower works independently without coordinating with neighboring growers.

items.text: Management of Tuta absoluta therefore cannot be limited to the boundaries of a single field; it requires area-wide management across the agricultural zone and the production community.
6. Tuta absoluta Under Egyptian Conditions

Egyptian conditions provide several factors that may help the pest persist, including warm weather during much of the year, multiple tomato-growing seasons, overlapping plantings, the presence of potato, eggplant, and other solanaceous hosts, and the retention of crop residues in some areas.
Movement of the pest from one growing season to another may maintain pest pressure throughout the season. Each field should therefore be viewed as part of a wider production system that includes:

items: Containers used to transport fruit and seedlings.

items.text: In open-field processing tomatoes, infestation may appear early and then increase as the canopy grows and plants overlap. If the canopy deteriorates at the end of the season, fruit becomes more exposed to heat and secondary damage while larvae continue attacking fruit close to harvest.
Fields that mature late may also be exposed to moth migration from earlier fields where harvesting has ended or control programs have stopped.
7. Why Is Chemical Control Alone Insufficient?

Global experience has shown that reliance on insecticides alone does not provide sustainable control of Tuta absoluta. The insect is protected inside mines during part of its life, and eggs, larvae, pupae, and adult moths may all be present in the field at the same time.
Repeated spraying may also lead to:

items: Fewer available options close to harvest.
Disruption of biological balance in the field.

items.text: Modern scientific reviews therefore emphasize that effective management of Tuta absoluta must be based on Integrated Pest Management (IPM), combining monitoring, traps, crop practices, biological control, and selective insecticides within a single program. [5, 6]

Applying IPM does not mean completely avoiding insecticides. It means using an insecticide when needed, at the correct time, with an appropriate active ingredient, and after assessing pest density, while preserving natural enemies as far as possible and preventing resistance development.
8. Initial Principles for Successful Pest Management
Before turning to the practical details in the following chapters, the general foundations of successful control can be summarized as follows:

items: Prevent the pest from entering with infested seedlings.
Install pheromone traps early, before severe infestation appears.
Inspect plants in the field and do not rely on traps alone.
Remove heavily infested leaves or plants at the beginning of an outbreak when feasible.
Dispose of crop residues promptly after harvest.
Prevent neglected fields from remaining a source of moths.
Use biological and microbial control agents within a clear program.
Select insecticides according to the active ingredient and mode of action, not merely the trade name.
Rotate insecticide groups according to the IRAC classification.
Comply with recommended rates and pre-harvest intervals.
Evaluate results by infestation levels and damaged fruit, not by the number of sprays.
Coordinate control among neighboring fields whenever possible.

items.text: 9. The Chapter’s Core Message
Tuta absoluta is not merely an insect that attacks tomato leaves. It is a complex economic pest affecting yield, quality, control costs, crop safety, and the sustainability of insecticide programs.
Its threat arises from its ability to:

items: Move rapidly between fields and regions.
Cause severe losses when detection is delayed.

items.text: Successful management therefore begins before economic damage appears, through prevention, monitoring, and early detection, followed by an integrated set of cultural, biological, behavioral, and chemical measures.
